title: Energy-Efficient Satellite Wake-Up via Bosonic Identification: The Role of Synchronization
abstract: The information-theoretic concept of identification describes a sender-receiver architecture in which the receiver only checks whether a particular message was sent or not, thereby promising a low-energy receiver design. In low received-energy regimes, quantum receivers are a promising tool for studying the system limits. However, the known information-theoretically optimal identification codes typically assume perfect synchronization. In this work, we study deterministic identification in a satellite setting under explicit synchronization constraints, where a satellite broadcasts the signature of a specific User Equipment (UE) which it assumes to be attached to one out of several possible Ground Station (GS), with the goal of establishing communication with the target UE. Within the proposed design, and assuming a specific phase-encoded coherent-state clock scheme in which the discrete time index is represented by equidistant phase rotations on the unit circle, our results reveal a fundamental asymmetry: At any transmission power, identification performance improves with blocklength, whereas synchronization accuracy degrades. In particular, the energy needed for transmitting the satellite clock to the GS can be several orders of magnitude higher than the one needed for the identification signal. This indicates that synchronization strongly impacts identification performance and motivates the investigation of the error-correcting capabilities of bosonic codes under jitter.
summary: This work analyzes deterministic identification in satellite wake-up systems constrained by energy. It reveals a critical conflict where reliable synchronization fails exponentially as the required signal blocklength increases. The authors propose that jointly optimizing for both tasks under a shared energy budget can mitigate this severe energy imbalance.
main_problem: The paper investigates the fundamental trade-off between achieving reliable signal identification and maintaining accurate clock synchronization in low-energy satellite wake-up communication systems.
main_result: There is a strong, fundamental asymmetry: while identification performance improves with blocklength, the required synchronization accuracy degrades exponentially with that same blocklength, leading to significant energy imbalances.
method: The analysis uses Markov chain modeling for clock drift and spectral decomposition of Toeplitz matrices to calculate the synchronization survival probability ($	ilde{P}_k$).
model_or_system: The system models a satellite broadcasting signals using phase-encoded coherent states to multiple Ground Stations (GS) attempting to wake up attached User Equipments (UEs). Synchronization is modeled as a discrete-time stochastic process.
key_observables: Identification performance vs. blocklength, synchronization survival probability ($	ilde{P}_k$), and the required energy for clock transmission versus identification signal transmission.
important_parameters: Blocklength ($k$), received energy levels ($E_{id}, E_{	ext{clock}}$), and the exponential decay rate governing $	ilde{P}_k$.
main_assumptions: The synchronization dynamics are modeled by a nearest-neighbor Markov chain, and the analysis assumes perfect correction if a deviation is detected.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the sharp threshold behavior of synchronization survival probability ($	ilde{P}_k$) versus clock energy, and compare the vastly different energy requirements for identification versus synchronization under independent vs. shared energy constraints.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the Markovian model for synchronization failure, deriving the exponential decay rate $	ilde{P}_k \sim 2^{-kI(a,T)}$, and then comparing optimization strategies: independent (leading to large energy gaps) versus joint optimization under a shared total energy budget.
why_interesting: The analysis provides quantitative bounds on the feasibility of quantum communication protocols in realistic, low-energy environments by explicitly modeling resource constraints (energy) against information-theoretic goals (identification).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Depicted is the situation with one satellite, roughly centered in the middle, and several GS with attached UEs. The GS set their receivers to detect if one of the signatures corresponding to a UE that is attached to them is transmitted by the satellite. A GS needs to track e.g. the satellite position in order to know the transmittivity, which is required for operation of the receiver as described in Sec. IV-B.

Fig. 2. Exact block synchronization survival probability ˜Pk as a function of the received clock energy. The identification operating point Eid yields negligible survival probability, while reliable synchronization requires a sig- nificantly larger clock energy E⋆ clock.

Fig. 4. Block synchronization survival probability ˜Pk as a function of the blocklength k at fixed identification energy Eid. The exponential decay with increasing k explains the failure of synchronization at the identification- optimal blocklength.

Fig. 3. Comparison of identification and required synchronization energies for the reference scenario. For each operating point, both received and transmitted energies per slot are shown. Synchronization requires several orders of magnitude more energy than identification.

Fig. 5. Identification-synchronization tradeoff as a function of the received identification energy Eid. Blue curves correspond to identification parameters optimized independently of synchronization; orange curves correspond to operation under a shared per-slot energy constraint. (a) Optimized blocklength. (b) Energy ratio Eclock/Eid, with vertical markers indicating Eclock = Eid. (c) Synchronization survival probability. (d) Total received energy.
